questions.question: A renewable energy plant can produce 800 megawatt-hours of wind power or 200 megawatt-hours of solar power per day. The plant increases solar output by 50 megawatt-hours. What is the opportunity cost in wind power?
options: 50 megawatt-hours, matching the increase in solar power production
200 megawatt-hours, reflecting the trade-off ratio of wind to solar power
400 megawatt-hours, representing half of the total wind power capacity
800 megawatt-hours, equal to the entire daily wind power production
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 solar unit = 800 ÷ 200 = 4 wind units. For 50 solar units: 50 × 4 = 200 megawatt-hours of wind power sacrificed.

questions.question: Country A produces a laptop in 15 hours and a smartphone in 3 hours. Country B produces a laptop in 20 hours and a smartphone in 5 hours. Which country has the absolute advantage in smartphones?
options: Country B, because its laptop and smartphone production times are closer
Country A, because it requires fewer labor hours to produce a smartphone
Country B, because its opportunity cost of smartphones is relatively lower
Neither country, because each leads in a different product's labor time
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage depends on fewer hours per unit. Country A needs 3 hours vs. 5 hours for a smartphone, making it the more efficient producer.

questions.question: Country North produces 100 units of Timber or 20 units of Gold. Country South produces 60 units of Timber or 15 units of Gold. Which country has the comparative advantage in Gold?
options: Country North, because it produces more Timber and Gold in absolute terms
Country South, because its opportunity cost of Gold is 4 Timber versus 5 for North
Country North, because its Gold output per worker exceeds South's Gold output
Country South, because it can produce Timber and Gold in nearly equal amounts
questions.explanation: North's cost of 1 Gold = 5 Timber (100/20). South's cost = 4 Timber (60/15). South has the lower relative cost in Gold.

questions.question: A nation's Production Possibility Frontier shifts outward to the right over a decade. What does this movement signify about the economy?
options: The economy is moving from an inefficient point to an efficient one
Economic growth through increased resources or technological advancement
A reallocation of resources from one sector to another within the economy
The economy is consuming beyond its production capacity through trade
questions.explanation: An outward PPF shift represents expanded productive capacity due to more resources, better technology, or improved productivity.

questions.question: Country X is 10% more productive than Country Y in Agriculture but 50% more productive in Manufacturing. In which sector does Country Y hold a comparative advantage?
options: Manufacturing, because Country X's productivity lead there is much larger
Agriculture, because Country Y is relatively less inefficient in that sector
Neither sector, because Country X has an absolute advantage in both goods
Both sectors equally, because productivity differences cancel each other out
questions.explanation: Country Y's productivity gap is smallest in Agriculture (10% vs. 50%). This is where its comparative disadvantage is least, giving it comparative advantage.

questions.question: In the Ricardian world relative supply curve, what determines the height at which the first horizontal step appears?
options: The average of the two countries' domestic opportunity cost ratios
The opportunity cost of the country with the lower relative cost for Good X
The world equilibrium price determined by the intersection with demand
The autarky price of Good X in the larger of the two trading countries
questions.explanation: The first step begins at the price where the most efficient producer of Good X is willing to supply it to the world market.

questions.question: The large country that currently produces both goods experiences a decline in its total labor force. How does this affect the World Relative Supply curve?
options: The vertical segment shifts upward as higher costs reduce relative supply
The horizontal length of the step associated with that country becomes shorter
The entire curve shifts to the right as smaller countries fill the supply gap
The curve is unaffected because relative supply depends on prices, not labor
questions.explanation: A smaller labor force reduces the maximum relative quantity that country can contribute to global supply at its given opportunity cost.

questions.question: Country A's productivity is 4 times that of Country B, while Country A's wage is 3 times that of Country B. Which country will export the good in the Ricardian model?
options: Country B, because its absolute wage level is lower and more competitive
Country A, because its productivity advantage exceeds its wage disadvantage
Both countries will export the good equally under these cost conditions
Neither country exports because the wage gap exactly offsets productivity
questions.explanation: Country A is more competitive because the productivity advantage (4×) exceeds the wage premium (3×). Country A has the lower unit labor cost.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, how does opening trade affect the real wage of workers when measured in terms of the good their country imports?
options: The real wage in terms of the imported good decreases as imports become costlier
The real wage in terms of the imported good increases because trade lowers its relative price
The real wage remains constant because it is determined solely by domestic MPL
The real wage fluctuates unpredictably depending on exchange rate movements
questions.explanation: Trade reduces the relative price of imported goods. Workers can buy more imports with their nominal earnings from the export sector.

questions.question: What is the magic of trade as described in the Ricardian model of international trade?
options: Trade eliminates all opportunity costs within each participating economy
The ability to consume a bundle of goods that lies beyond the domestic PPF
Trade guarantees that every worker in every country receives higher wages
The automatic equalization of all factor prices across every trading nation
questions.explanation: By specializing according to comparative advantage and trading, nations can consume combinations of goods outside their own production possibilities.

questions.question: In a many-good Ricardian model, how is the cutoff point between exported and imported goods determined?
options: By the absolute productivity level in each sector averaged across countries
By comparing relative labor requirements to the relative wage ratio between nations
By government trade policy that designates certain goods as strategic exports
By consumer preferences that determine which foreign goods are in highest demand
questions.explanation: A country exports goods where the ratio of its productivity advantage exceeds the ratio of its wage level compared to trading partners.

questions.question: Why does the compensation principle justify trade liberalization even when it causes some workers to lose their jobs?
options: Job losses from trade are always temporary and reverse within several months
Total gains to winners are large enough to theoretically compensate all losers fully
International law requires governments to compensate every displaced worker
Workers who lose jobs from trade always find higher-paying employment elsewhere
questions.explanation: Trade is a potential Pareto improvement. The net social surplus increases, meaning winners could compensate losers and still remain better off.

questions.question: A resource reallocation helps 5 people and harms no one, while leaving 1,000 other people completely unaffected. Is this a move toward Pareto Efficiency?
options: No, because too few people benefit relative to the total population size
Yes, because at least one person gains and absolutely no one is made worse off
No, because the 1,000 unaffected people must also gain for it to qualify
Yes, but only if the 5 beneficiaries compensate the 1,000 unaffected people
questions.explanation: A Pareto Improvement requires only that some gain and none lose. The number of people affected is irrelevant to the Pareto criterion.

questions.question: The price of Cloth is \$30 with a unit labor requirement of 3. The price of Wine is \$20 with a unit labor requirement of 1. With mobile labor, where do workers go?
options: To the Cloth sector, because the price per unit of Cloth is higher than Wine
To the Wine sector, because the hourly wage of \$20 exceeds Cloth's wage of \$10
Workers split evenly because both sectors offer identical hourly earnings
To the Cloth sector, because producing fewer units signals higher skill required
questions.explanation: Wage in Cloth = \$30/3 = \$10. Wage in Wine = \$20/1 = \$20. Workers migrate to the higher-paying Wine sector.

questions.question: What does the slope of the Ricardian PPF represent in an autarky equilibrium?
options: The world relative price ratio established through international trade
The domestic relative price of the good measured on the horizontal axis
The ratio of total labor supply to the total capital stock in the economy
The marginal rate of substitution between the two goods for all consumers
questions.explanation: In autarky equilibrium, the relative price must equal the ratio of marginal costs, which is the slope of the PPF (a_LX / a_LY).

questions.question: A country has 500 units of total labor and the unit labor requirement for Good X is a_LX = 5. What is the maximum possible production of Good X?
options: 2,500 units, calculated by multiplying total labor by the labor requirement
100 units, found by dividing total available labor by the unit labor requirement
500 units, matching the total labor endowment available in the country
5 units, reflecting the inverse of the unit labor requirement times labor
questions.explanation: Maximum output = Total Labor ÷ Unit Labor Requirement = 500 ÷ 5 = 100 units of Good X.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the export good's price rises by 20%. What happens to the real income of capital owners in that export sector?
options: It increases by 20% in terms of the other good while staying constant in terms of its own
It decreases because higher export prices raise the overall cost of living in the economy
It remains unchanged because real returns are fixed regardless of nominal price changes
It falls in terms of both goods as the price increase is offset by higher import costs
questions.explanation: Nominal income rises by 20%. Since the other good's price is unchanged, purchasing power over it increases. Real income in terms of the export good itself stays constant.

questions.question: What is the key short-run assumption about capital in the immobile factor model?
options: Capital can move freely and instantly between any sectors of the economy
Capital is specific to its current industry and cannot be repurposed or relocated
Capital is the only mobile factor while labor is fixed in each industry
Capital does not exist in the model because labor is the sole factor of production
questions.explanation: This short-run immobility of capital distinguishes the model from the long-run Heckscher-Ohlin framework where all factors are mobile.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, which group has the strongest incentive to lobby the government for trade protection?
options: Export sector workers who want to expand their access to foreign markets
Both labor and capital owners together in the import-competing sector
Consumers who benefit from the lower prices that trade protection brings
Foreign firms seeking guaranteed access to the protected domestic market
questions.explanation: Both factors in the import-competing sector suffer income losses from trade that they cannot escape. They have a shared interest in protection.

questions.question: Country A has 100 Capital and 200 Labor. Country B has 50 Capital and 50 Labor. According to the H-O definition, which country is capital-abundant?
options: Country A, because it possesses a larger absolute quantity of capital stock
Country B, because its capital-to-labor ratio of 1.0 exceeds Country A's ratio of 0.5
Country A, because its total labor force is four times larger than Country B's
Neither country, because both have more labor than capital in absolute terms
questions.explanation: Factor abundance is relative, not absolute. Country B's K/L = 50/50 = 1.0. Country A's K/L = 100/200 = 0.5. Country B is relatively capital-abundant.

questions.question: The Stolper-Samuelson Theorem suggests that trade liberalization in a labor-abundant developing country will have what effect on income inequality?
options: Increase inequality because trade always benefits the wealthy capital owners
Reduce inequality if labor is the lower-income group and their real wages rise
Have no effect on inequality because trade benefits all factors proportionally
Worsen inequality by reducing the real wages of all workers across the economy
questions.explanation: Trade raises the return to the abundant factor (labor) and lowers the return to the scarce factor (capital). If labor is the poorer group, inequality narrows.

questions.question: The price of the capital-intensive good rises by 10%. According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, what happens to the rental rate of capital?
options: It rises by exactly 10%, matching the product price increase proportionally
It rises by more than 10% due to the Magnification Effect on factor returns
It rises by less than 10% because some gains go to the mobile factor labor
It remains unchanged because capital returns are determined by technology alone
questions.explanation: The Magnification Effect ensures that the return to the factor used intensively in the good whose price rose increases by more than the price change itself.

questions.question: What does the endowment effect refer to in the Heckscher-Ohlin model of trade?
options: Countries with more total resources trade less because they are more self-sufficient
Larger resource bases increase trade volume but the pattern follows factor ratios
The initial distribution of resources determines which goods become non-traded
Resource endowments have no effect on trade once technology is accounted for
questions.explanation: Factor ratios determine which goods a country exports. Absolute endowment sizes determine how much trade occurs but not the pattern of comparative advantage.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, P_M rises, drawing labor from Agriculture into Manufacturing. When does this labor movement stop?
options: When all available labor has moved entirely into the Manufacturing sector
When the value of the marginal product of labor is equal in both sectors
When the government imposes restrictions on further labor reallocation
When agricultural wages fall low enough to force farmers out of business
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is reached when P_M × MPL_M = P_A × MPL_A. At this point, there is no further incentive for labor to switch sectors.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, P_M rises. What happens to the rental rate of Land in the Agricultural sector?
options: The rental rate of land increases because higher wages boost demand for food
The rental rate of land decreases as labor leaves and marginal product falls
The rental rate remains constant because land is a fixed and immobile factor
The rental rate initially rises as food prices adjust, then returns to normal
questions.explanation: As labor leaves agriculture for manufacturing, the marginal product of the remaining fixed land declines, reducing its rental return.

questions.question: Why is the change in the real wage described as ambiguous when the export good's price rises in the Specific Factor Model?
options: The nominal wage always falls while both goods prices rise simultaneously
The nominal wage rises less than the export price but more than the constant import price
Real wages are never affected by price changes in the Specific Factor Model
The effect depends entirely on government policy rather than market forces
questions.explanation: Purchasing power rises in terms of imports (whose price is unchanged) but falls in terms of exports. The net effect depends on the worker's consumption basket.

questions.question: A country receives a large gift of new Capital equipment for its Manufacturing sector in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the nominal wage?
options: The nominal wage decreases because capital replaces workers in production
The nominal wage increases as more capital raises labor's marginal product
The nominal wage remains constant because wages depend only on food prices
The nominal wage falls in manufacturing but rises in the agricultural sector
questions.explanation: More capital increases the MPL in manufacturing, shifting labor demand outward and raising wages across both sectors as labor moves toward manufacturing.

questions.question: What is the fundamental distinction between internal and external economies of scale?
options: Internal applies to domestic firms while external applies to foreign competitors
Internal depends on the firm's own output; external depends on the industry's total output
Internal economies are always larger in magnitude than external economies
There is no meaningful distinction; both terms describe the same phenomenon
questions.explanation: Internal economies arise from a firm's own scale of production. External economies arise from the size of the entire industry in a given location.

questions.question: In the Krugman model, trade increases the size of the integrated market. What happens to firms' average production costs?
options: Average cost rises because firms must invest in more product differentiation
Average cost decreases as fixed costs are spread across a larger customer base
Average cost remains constant because technology does not change with trade
Average cost initially rises as firms enter, then falls as competition intensifies
questions.explanation: Access to a larger global market allows each firm to increase output, spreading fixed R&D and setup costs over more units sold.

questions.question: Why does New Trade Theory suggest that historical accidents can permanently determine which countries export which goods?
options: International law protects the trade patterns established by early industrializers
Early industry leaders build external economies that create barriers to new entrants
Consumer preferences are permanently fixed by the first products they encounter
Governments permanently subsidize the industries that developed first historically
questions.explanation: Once a cluster achieves scale (like Silicon Valley), its accumulated cost advantages and specialized ecosystem make it extremely difficult for new regions to compete.

questions.question: What is the terms-of-trade gain that a large country can achieve by imposing a tariff on imports?
options: The additional government revenue collected from domestic consumers at the border
The reduction in the pre-tariff price that foreign exporters must accept to maintain sales
The increase in domestic producer surplus as protected firms expand their output
The improvement in product quality as foreign firms compete for market access
questions.explanation: By reducing its import demand, a large country forces foreign suppliers to lower their prices. This transfer from foreign exporters is the terms-of-trade gain.

questions.question: For a small country, which trade policy instrument typically generates the largest efficiency loss or deadweight loss?
options: A revenue tariff applied to goods that are not produced domestically at all
An export subsidy, which distorts both production and consumption while costing revenue
An import quota with licenses that are competitively auctioned by the government
A voluntary export restraint negotiated with principal foreign supplying countries
questions.explanation: An export subsidy creates production and consumption distortions and requires direct government expenditure, with no offsetting terms-of-trade benefit for a small country.

questions.question: How does the national welfare effect of a Voluntary Export Restraint compare to that of an equivalent import tariff?
options: The VER is better because it avoids direct government intervention in markets
The VER is worse because quota rents are captured by foreign exporting firms
Both policies have identical welfare effects when restricting the same import volume
The VER is better for large countries while tariffs are better for small countries
questions.explanation: Under a tariff, the government collects the revenue. Under a VER, foreign exporters capture the scarcity rent through higher prices, worsening the welfare outcome for the importing country.

questions.question: What distinguishes a selective quota from a global quota in international trade policy?
options: Selective quotas are set by the WTO while global quotas are set unilaterally
A selective quota restricts imports from specific countries rather than all sources
Global quotas apply to all goods while selective quotas target only one product
Selective quotas are always more restrictive than global quotas at the same level
questions.explanation: A selective quota limits imports from particular named trading partners, while a global quota sets one total limit regardless of country of origin.

questions.question: A \$50 specific tariff is applied to an imported good with a world price of \$500. What is the ad valorem equivalent rate of this tariff?
options: 5%, calculated as the tariff divided by the domestic price after the tax
10%, equal to the specific tariff amount divided by the world price of the good
\$50, because specific tariffs are always expressed in absolute dollar terms
50%, reflecting the ratio of the world price to the specific tariff amount
questions.explanation: Ad valorem equivalent = Specific tariff ÷ World price = \$50 ÷ \$500 = 0.10, or 10% of the good's value.

questions.question: In the small country case of tariff analysis, which groups are considered the winners after a tariff is imposed?
options: Domestic consumers and foreign exporters, who benefit from higher trade volumes
Domestic producers and the government, through higher prices and tariff revenue
Foreign producers and domestic consumers, who gain from market price stability
All domestic groups benefit equally because tariffs protect the national economy
questions.explanation: Domestic producers receive higher protected prices. The government collects tariff revenue. Consumers lose surplus, and total efficiency declines.

questions.question: A final good has a 5% nominal tariff. Inputs enter duty-free and represent 80% of the final good's total cost. What is the Effective Rate of Protection?
options: 5%, matching the nominal tariff rate on the final assembled good
25%, calculated as the nominal tariff divided by the domestic value-added share
1%, because most of the product's value comes from untaxed imported inputs
80%, reflecting the share of costs that enter the country without any tariff
questions.explanation: ERP = Nominal tariff ÷ Value-added share = 0.05 ÷ (1 – 0.80) = 0.05 ÷ 0.20 = 25% effective protection for domestic assembly.

questions.question: Why does a tariff create a production distortion in the domestic economy?
options: It forces consumers to pay higher prices for goods they previously purchased
It encourages domestic firms to produce goods at costs above the world price
It reduces government revenue that could fund public infrastructure projects
It causes foreign firms to exit the market, permanently reducing competition
questions.explanation: The tariff artificially inflates the domestic price above the true opportunity cost (the world price), causing inefficient allocation of domestic productive resources.

questions.question: What is administrative protection as a non-tariff barrier to international trade?
options: Government departments that directly manage and operate import-competing firms
The use of complex customs procedures and regulations to discourage or slow imports
A formal system where bureaucrats set import quotas on a case-by-case basis
International administrative tribunals that adjudicate trade disputes between nations
questions.explanation: Administrative protection raises costs for foreign firms through bureaucratic hurdles, paperwork requirements, and regulatory complexity rather than explicit taxes or quotas.

questions.question: How does an import quota's effect on domestic prices compare to that of an equivalent tariff when domestic demand increases?
options: Prices rise by the same amount under both policies because they are equivalent
The quota can lead to much higher prices because the import supply is absolutely capped
Tariffs cause larger price increases because they directly add a tax to each unit
Prices fall under a quota as domestic producers expand output to meet demand
questions.explanation: Under a tariff, additional imports can enter at the taxed world price. Under a quota, the import volume is fixed, so demand increases drive domestic prices sharply higher.

questions.question: Which trade policy instrument is most commonly associated with the infant industry argument for protection in developing nations?
options: Export subsidies that help domestic firms sell their products in foreign markets
Import quotas or high tariffs that shield young domestic firms from foreign competition
Voluntary export restraints negotiated with more advanced industrial countries
Government procurement policies that favor domestic suppliers for public contracts
questions.explanation: The infant industry argument justifies temporary import restrictions to allow new domestic firms to grow and achieve competitive economies of scale.

questions.question: What is the social cost of rent-seeking behavior associated with import quota systems?
options: Higher consumer prices caused by the reduced supply of imported goods to the market
Real resources spent on lobbying that could have been directed to productive investment
The government revenue lost because quotas do not automatically generate tax income
Reduced product variety as foreign firms exit the restricted domestic market
questions.explanation: Firms spend valuable resources trying to capture quota rents through political influence. These resources are wasted from society's perspective because they create no new output.

questions.question: A country has a trade deficit of \$50 billion, net factor income of –\$10 billion, and net unilateral transfers of +\$5 billion. What is the Current Account balance?
options: +\$35 billion, reflecting the surplus from net transfers offsetting the deficits
–\$55 billion, the sum of the trade deficit, negative factor income, and transfers
–\$45 billion, calculated by netting the transfers against the factor income only
–\$65 billion, adding all negative entries and ignoring the positive transfers
questions.explanation: Current Account = Trade balance + Net factor income + Net transfers = (–\$50B) + (–\$10B) + \$5B = –\$55B.

questions.question: What does a country's Net International Investment Position measure at a given point in time?
options: The annual flow of foreign investment moving into and out of the country
The stock of foreign assets owned domestically minus foreign liabilities owed abroad
The difference between exports and imports of investment-related services
The total value of the central bank's official foreign exchange reserve holdings
questions.explanation: NIIP is a stock measure capturing a country's net external wealth: domestically owned foreign assets minus foreign-owned domestic assets.

questions.question: A country's Balance of Payments shows a Financial Account surplus. What does this indicate about its international financial position?
options: The country is a net lender, providing capital to the rest of the world
The country is a net borrower, receiving more capital inflows than outflows
The country has a Current Account surplus of exactly the same magnitude
The central bank is accumulating foreign exchange reserves rapidly
questions.explanation: A Financial Account surplus means more capital is flowing into the country than out. The country is borrowing from or selling assets to foreigners on net.

questions.question: What types of cross-border income flows are recorded in the Primary Income account of the Balance of Payments?
options: Exports and imports of manufactured goods and commercial services
Investment earnings like interest and dividends, plus compensation of employees abroad
Foreign aid payments, charitable donations, and migrant worker remittances
The purchase and sale of financial assets like stocks and government bonds
questions.explanation: Primary income records returns to factors of production across borders: investment income (interest, dividends) and labor income (compensation of employees).

questions.question: The US Dollar appreciates by 10% against the Euro, with no other changes in the market. What happens to the Dollar price of German cars sold in the United States?
options: The price increases because a stronger Dollar makes foreign goods more valuable
The price decreases because each Dollar can now purchase more Euro-denominated goods
The price remains the same because car prices are set in long-term contracts
The price initially rises but then falls as exchange rates return to equilibrium
questions.explanation: A stronger Dollar buys more Euros. German cars priced in Euros become cheaper for American consumers when converted to Dollars.

questions.question: What is exchange rate overshooting in international macroeconomic models?
options: When the central bank deliberately pushes the exchange rate past its target level
When the short-run exchange rate moves beyond its long-run equilibrium after a monetary shock
When the nominal exchange rate changes but the real exchange rate stays constant
When speculative attacks force a currency far below its fundamental market value
questions.explanation: Overshooting occurs because goods prices are sticky in the short run while financial markets adjust instantly. The exchange rate must temporarily over-adjust.

questions.question: What is the core prediction of Purchasing Power Parity theory about exchange rates?
options: Exchange rates are determined entirely by government monetary policy decisions
Identical goods should cost the same amount in different countries when expressed in a common currency
Currencies always depreciate over time because of continuous inflation in all countries
Exchange rates fluctuate randomly and cannot be predicted by any economic theory
questions.explanation: PPP is based on the Law of One Price. It predicts that exchange rates adjust to equalize the purchasing power of different currencies over the long run.

questions.question: Under a fixed exchange rate regime, the central bank must buy its own domestic currency to defend the peg. What happens to the domestic money supply?
options: The money supply increases as the central bank injects liquidity into markets
The money supply decreases as domestic currency is removed from circulation
The money supply remains unchanged because sterilization offsets the intervention
The money supply depends entirely on the fiscal policy stance, not exchange rate policy
questions.explanation: Buying domestic currency (taking it out of circulation) and selling foreign reserves reduces the domestic monetary base, contracting the money supply.
